coredata.dc:title: In situ synthesis of carbon quantum dots on carbon felt fibers as a new solid-phase microextraction sorbent for chromatographic analysis of bisphenol A
coredata.dc:creator: Sheikhi, Tahereh
Razmi, Habib
Mohammadiazar, Sirwan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an innovative and efficient solid − phase microextraction (SPME) sorbent was introduced based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified carbon felt (CF) substrate. CF, due to its unique characteristics including high porosity, solvent durability (without swelling), and simple modification, it was proposed as an attractive platform for preconcentration purposes. To create an impressive adsorption capacity, the synergistic effects of CQDs and the carbonaceous structure of CF were profited off. CQDs were successfully synthesized on CF fibers by using an improved one-step hydrothermal method, and the resulting sorbent was referred to as CQDs@CF. Since bisphenol A (BPA) is an environmental toxin and can disrupt the endocrine system and potentially lead to cancer, highlighting the importance of detecting its presence. The prepared sorbent was used to extract bisphenol A (BPA) as a model compound from the real samples, followed by HPLC − UV analysis. Extraction parameters including desorption solvent type, and its volume, extraction, and desorption time, salting out effect and, solution pH were optimized. FESEM, EDX, FT − IR, and N2 adsorption–desorption were used to characterize the sorbent morphology, structure, and elemental contents. Under optimal conditions, the response linearity in the range of 0.05 − 500 ng mL−1 and detection limit at the level of 0.01 ng mL−1 were obtained. Additionally, the relative standard deviations (RSD) for newly prepared CQDs@CF (sorbent to sorbent repeatability) and a certain multi − used CQDs@CF (single sorbent repeatability) at a concentration of 100 ng mL−1 of BPA, were found to be 7.2 and 4.8, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon felt
Carbon quantum dots
Solid phase microextraction
Bisphenol A
Chromatographic analysis
One-step hydrothermal synthesis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical figures of merit of the method
Comparison of the proposed method with others

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks to Azerbaijan Shahid Madani University for financial support of this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE − SEM images of CF at different stages of treatment: pristine CF (a1 − a3), acid-treated CF (b1 − b3), and synthesized CQDs@CF (c1 − c3).

EDX spectra of pristine CF (a1), acid-treated CF (b1), CQDs@CF (c1), and EDX mapping analysis of the individual elements along with corresponding FE − SEM images for pristine CF (a2 − a4), acid-treated CF (b2 − b4), and CQDs@CF (c2 − c4), respectively.
FT − IR spectra of pristine CF (a), acid-treated CF (b), and synthesized CQDs@CF (c).
BET plots of pristine CF (a), acid-treated CF (b), CQDs@CF (c), and BJH plots of pristine CF (d), acid-treated CF (e), and CQDs@CF (f).

Influence of the (A) sorbent type, (B) citric acid amounts as CQD precursor, (C) extraction time, (D) desorption solvent type, (E) desorption solvent volume, (F) desorption time, (G) NaCl%, and (H) solution pH on the peak areas of BPA.
Chromatograms obtained by using CQDs@CF sorbent for non-spiked bottled water sample (I) and the same sample spiked with 10 ng mL−1 of target analysts (II).
The result of the BET plot of pristine CF (a), acid-treated CF (b), and CQDs@CF (c).
Analytical figures of merit of the developed method utilizing CQDs@CF sorbent.
Determination of BPA in different real samples using CQDs@CF sorbent and corresponding relative recovery results (replicate = 3).
Comparison of the efficiency of the proposed method based on CQDs@CF SPME sorbent with other BPA determination methods.

row.entry: Bottled water, packed cup water, packed milk, and thermal paper

head.ce:title: In situ synthesis of carbon quantum dots on carbon felt fibers as a new solid-phase microextraction sorbent for chromatographic analysis of bisphenol A

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 53714-161, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 53714-161, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Islamic Azad University, Sanandaj Branch, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Islamic Azad University, Sanandaj Branch, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an innovative and efficient solid − phase microextraction (SPME) sorbent was introduced based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified carbon felt (CF) substrate. CF, due to its unique characteristics including high porosity, solvent durability (without swelling), and simple modification, it was proposed as an attractive platform for preconcentration purposes. To create an impressive adsorption capacity, the synergistic effects of CQDs and the carbonaceous structure of CF were profited off. CQDs were successfully synthesized on CF fibers by using an improved one-step hydrothermal method, and the resulting sorbent was referred to as CQDs@CF. Since bisphenol A (BPA) is an environmental toxin and can disrupt the endocrine system and potentially lead to cancer, highlighting the importance of detecting its presence. The prepared sorbent was used to extract bisphenol A (BPA) as a model compound from the real samples, followed by HPLC − UV analysis. Extraction parameters including desorption solvent type, and its volume, extraction, and desorption time, salting out effect and, solution pH were optimized. FESEM, EDX, FT − IR, and N2 adsorption–desorption were used to characterize the sorbent morphology, structure, and elemental contents. Under optimal conditions, the response linearity in the range of 0.05 − 500 ng mL−1 and detection limit at the level of 0.01 ng mL−1 were obtained. Additionally, the relative standard deviations (RSD) for newly prepared CQDs@CF (sorbent to sorbent repeatability) and a certain multi − used CQDs@CF (single sorbent repeatability) at a concentration of 100 ng mL−1 of BPA, were found to be 7.2 and 4.8, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination caused by headstrong organics such as plastic and pharmaceuticals has become a natural issue genuinely influencing human well-being and biological systems [1]. Owing to their high toxicity, rather stable, and low biodegradability molecular structures, these organics are difficult to remove by conventional treatment methods [2]. For example, as one of the most active endocrine disruptors, bisphenol A (BPA) can be mentioned, which can mimic the function of estradiol, and cause adverse effects on human health [3]. Bisphenol A is a synthetic organic compound with the chemical formula (CH3)2C(C6H4OH)2, also known as 2,2 − bis(4 − hydroxyphenyl) propane [4]. It is used in the production of polyesters, polysulfones, and polyether ketones and serves as a key building block in the production of polycarbonate plastics (PC) and epoxy resins [5]. Many everyday items, including medical and dental tools, sealants and dental fillings, eyeglass lenses, sports gear, water and milk bottles, and household appliances are produced using polycarbonate plastic [6]. It is also used in the thermal paper used in-store checkouts, which is also absorbed through the skin [7]. However, BPA is known to be an endocrine-disrupting chemical (EDC) that can interfere with various health issues including obesity, diabetes, and reproductive disorders [8]. Also, unfortunately, studies have shown that BPA has potent carcinogenic effects [9]. Therefore, measurement and diagnosis in different matrices are very important for public health and environmental protection [10]. In addition, the estrogenic effects of BPA have been the subject of extensive research [11], but its isolation and trace level detection in complex matrices have been challenging until yet [12]. Proper sample preparation method before analysis is, therefore, very important to eliminate interferences and improve the sensitivity of analytical methods in this regard. Since first proposed in 1990 by Arthur and Pawliszyn, SPME has undergone rapid developments [13]. The SPME method has become a suitable choice for applications in food, environmental, and life sciences; and has attracted enormous interest in the fields of bioscience, environmental, and pharmaceutical analyses due to its excellent capabilities like the possibility of combination with various sampling techniques and miniaturization of instruments meeting the requirements of green chemistry [14,15]. Although commercially available SPME coatings such as PA, PDMS, and DVB/PDMS are widely used, however, more effort is required to design new materials aimed at the trace amounts analysis of specific compounds.

In recent decades, researchers have focused on developing advanced coatings for SPME, such as metal oxide composites [16,17], metal–organic frameworks [18], covalent organic frameworks [19], ionic liquids [20], and carbon nanomaterials [21,22]. Carbon-based nanomaterials have received much attention in recent years due to their unique characteristics, including ease of synthesis, high selectivity to the desired analytes, and excellent sensitivity, as well as high dispersibility in water, compatibility with the environment, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and excellent photostability [23].

An important group of advanced carbonaceous materials developed as coating materials for SPME is carbon quantum dots (CQDs). This material exhibits excellent adsorption and extraction performance due to its large specific surface area, rich π − electron structure, and high hydrophobicity. CQDs have very small sizes and high surface − to − volume ratios and contain oxygen − containing groups such as hydroxyl, epoxide, carbonyl, and carboxyl on their surfaces. These properties make them potentially effective for adsorbing organic compounds through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic attraction, and π-π interactions [24]. CQDs contain many polar moieties such as hydroxyl, epoxide, and carboxyl groups, making them highly dispersible in aqueous solutions. Surface functionalization can further alter physical properties, including solubility in aqueous and non − aqueous solvents [25] appropriating them for use in extraction applications [26].

Despite the potential of quantum dots (QDs), their application in extraction and preconcentration is limited due to the difficulty in the separation of analyte-loaded adsorbent nanoparticles. A facile route to overcome the problem is magnetizing them with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles. For example, N-doped carbon quantum dot conjugated with Fe3O4 as a green and facial adsorbent [27] for preconcentration and extraction of lead ions in vegetable and water samples, and titanium oxide nanotubes modified with CuFe2O4 quantum dots for solid − phase extraction of uranium [28] were reported. Additionally, magnetic graphene quantum dots) GQDs) have been used as adsorbents to measure bisphenol A in water samples [29]. The strong hydrophilicity of CQDs limited their application for the preconcentration process from the viewpoint of difficulty in separation and recycling. To solve this problem, some support materials have been used to immobilize hydrophilic CQDs. In the separation process, in addition, carbon-based materials especially three-dimensional CF, are the proper choice for preparing SPME sorbent for several reasons including low cost, excellent separation efficiency, high surface area, and a porous structure [30]. CF is often seen as long straight filaments that are randomly scattered with large homogeneous voids between them. As reported by Gonzalez − Garcia et al., each fiber has a compartmental shape composed of narrower filaments [31].

The present study aimed to construct an SPME sorbent coated with CQDs using in − situ hydrothermal synthesis methods. This new sorbent was designed for microextraction and trace determination of BPA. The properties of the sorbent, including extraction efficiency, chemical stability, and lifespan were evaluated. To assess the applicability of the developed method to real sample analysis, the CQDs@CF was used to extract BPA in bottled water, packed cup water, packed milk, and thermal paper samples, and determined by HPLC − UV technique.

In this work, citric acid (CA) (C6H8O7, 99.5 %) was purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). In contrast, analytical grade ethanol, acetone, n − hexane, and nitric acid (65 %) were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). To prepare the standard solution (in methanol with a concentration of 1000 mg L−1 and stored at 4 °C), BPA was obtained from Sigma − Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). BPA working solution was prepared daily by appropriately diluting the stock solution with deionized water. All solvents used in this study, including acetonitrile and methanol, were his HPLC grade and purchased from Carlo Erba (Vaudreuil, France). The CF was supplied by the Beijing Great Wall Co. Ltd. The bottled water, packed cup water, packed milk samples, and thermal paper samples were prepared from the supermarkets, restaurants, and chain stores at Azerbaijan Shahid Madani University.

Analysis was performed using high − performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (JASCO HPLC, Japan). HSS 2000 software (JASCO, Japan) controlled the chromatograph through LC − Net II/ADC interface and data processor (BORWIN software, version 1.50, Japan). Separations were performed using an ODS − 3 precolumn (10 mm x 4 mm ID, 5 μm) and an ODS − 3 column (250 mm x 4.6 mm ID, 5 μm) (MZ − Analysentechnik, Germany). The system used consisted of an isocratic pump (PU − 1580), an injector (Rheodyne 7725i) with a 20 μL stainless steel sample loop (Rheodyne, Cotati, CA, USA), and a UV–1575 spectrophotometric detector. The sample was injected into the HPLC system using a 25 µL microsyringe ®Hamilton Company (Bonaduz Switzerland).

The fiber surface was characterized using an electronic field emission scanning microscope (FESEM) model MIRA III (Tescan, Brno, Czech Republic) and an Energy − dispersive X − ray spectroscope (EDS) model MIRA II SAMX detector (made in France). Using a measurement and degassing system (BEL, Japan), BET and BJH analyses were performed to examine the surface properties of the original CF, acid-treated CF, and CQD@CF. The presence of functional groups and/or chemical bonds on the various examined sorbents was monitored by FT − IR spectroscopy in the range of 500 − 4000 cm−1 using (Bruker VECORT 22 spectrophotometer, Germany). Sonication was carried out with a FALC ultrasonic model LBS2 (Italy) and the pH of the solutions was measured with a Metrohm pH meter model 744 (Switzerland).

To prepare the CQDs@CF sorbent, first, a 20 × 5 mm piece of CF with a 2 mm thickness was prepared and then sonicated in a mixture of ethanol and deionized water (4:1) to remove impurities and unwanted chemicals. Then, to make an acid-treated CF sorbent, it was placed in 65 % nitric acid for 40 min to activate the surface of the carbon nanofibers and create more hydrophilicity. CQDs@CF was synthesized via a hydrothermal method in a one-step, according to a previously published method with some modifications [32]. Specifically, 0.5 g of CA was added to 20 mL of deionized water and sonicated along with the activated CF for 5 min to an efficient interaction between CA and CF fibers. The moist and CA-impregnated CF was then transferred to a 50 ml Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave together with the CA solution at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to laboratory temperature, the synthesized CQDs@CF was removed from the autoclave containing light-brown solution, confirming the successful CQDs formation reaction. Finally, the CQDs@CF were washed several times with distilled water and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 30 min before use as solid phase microextraction sorbent.

A 25 mL working standard solution of BPA at the concentration level of 25 ng mL−1 was prepared daily from a stock solution of 1000 ng mL−1. The pH of the sample solution was adjusted to 6 by diluted HCl or NaOH solution. Afterward, CQDs@CF sorbent was thoroughly immersed in the working solution for 20 min to extract the analyte, then the sorbent was removed from the sample solution and immediately immersed in 1.5 mL methanol as a desorbing solvent for 15 min. After deriving out of the sorbent, methanol was evaporated by the N2 stream, and the BPA residual was then re-dissolved by adding 100 µL methanol. The isocratic elution mode was performed with a mobile phase consisting of acetonitrile: water (45:55 v/v) at a flow rate of 1 mL/min. The HPLC − UV detector wavelength was set to 224 nm. Scheme 1 shows the schematic diagram of the proposed method. Real samples including bottled water, packed cup water, packed milk, and thermal paper were analyzed from the viewpoint of BPA content by the proposed method. Method validation was also performed by relative recovery tests.

Real samples of bottled water, packed cup water, packed milk, and thermal paper were selected for BPA analysis using the proposed method. Various extraction methods for BPA from packed milk, bottled water, packed cup water, and thermal paper samples are described [5,33–35]. According to these reports, to extract BPA from paper samples, 100 mg of thermal paper sample were cut into small pieces of approximately 3 × 3 mm, and 10 mL of mixed methanol: water (50:50) was added to it and ultrasound-assisted extraction was carried out. Next, the solution was centrifuged at a speed of 5000 rpm and the supernatant was filtered, and then 3 mL of it was diluted with distilled water 25 times. In addition, the extract was filtered by a syringe filter head before the SPME experiment and HPLC − UV analysis. For milk samples, 3.2 mL of acetonitrile was added to 600 μL of milk from the packaged milk sample, followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm. For defatting, the supernatant was transferred into a glass tube and placed in a freezer for 6 h. The supernatant was filtered and 3 mL of it was diluted with distilled water 25 times. The mineral water samples kept in polyethylene bottles and cups were used to estimate the amount of BPA leakage from polyethylene plastic containers by the proposed SPME-based chromatographic analysis method. Finally, the relative recoveries and their confidence levels were calculated to represent method accuracy and precision. In all cases, spiked samples were prepared as real sample solutions, by adding a certain amount of BPA (10.00 and 50.00 ng mL−1) to the sample before the extraction stage.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE − SEM), and energy − dispersive X − ray spectroscopy (EDX) were performed to characterize the surface morphology and elemental distribution of the pristine CF, acid − treated CF, and CQDs@CF which were shown in Fig. 1 (a1 − a3), (b1 − b3), and (c1 − c3), respectively. In terms of morphology, acid − treated CF and pristine CF are not much different, but porous structures and increased surface area were observed in the case of CQDs@CF, leading to increased absorption properties of the sorbent and thus improved efficiency of the sample preparation method.

The CF and its modified forms, acid-treated CF and CQD@CF, were demonstrated by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis (Fig. 2 ) representing carbon and oxygen elements in the investigated sorbents structures. Meanwhile, the distribution density and compression of elemental carbon was decreased by modification processes, probably due to the removal of weakly bonded carbon microfibers from the CF structure while washing it in an acidic medium (comparison of a2, b2, in Fig. 2). In addition, it seems that the compression of carbon microfibers was also decreased through the hydrothermal operations. So, the elemental carbon distribution density was not compensated by the formation of CQDs on the carbon fibers (Fig. 2, (c2)). Elemental oxygen changes in the case of CQDs@CF were investigated compared to CF or acid-treated CF, which indicates an increased oxygen density (Fig. 2 (c3)). This may be attributed to oxygen-containing functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl, epoxy, and so on). This result is in agreement with other work [36].

The presence of functional groups and/or chemical bonds in the CQDs@CF sorbent was monitored by the FT − IR technique. In addition, Fig. 3 shows the FT − IR spectra of a typical pristine CF (a), acid − treated CF (b), and CQDs@CF(c). O − H bonds in various functional groups such as alcohols, phenols [36], carboxylic acids, and amines reported at 3000 − 3500. The wide adsorption bond at 3334 cm−1 is generally associated with the stretching vibrations of the O − H bonds belonged to hydroxyl and carboxylic groups. The peak at 2918 − 2853 cm −1 was attributed to the aliphatic C − H, and the C = O groups were identified from the peaks at 1693 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1. The aromatic C = C bonds were confirmed by the peak at 1595 cm−1, furthermore, the peak located at 1245 cm−1 could be assigned to the C − O bond. IR absorption bonds localized over the range 600 − 700 cm−1 dealt with the C − C bending vibrations for small alkyl groups on the CQDs@CF [36].

To achieve the best analytical performance in BPA analysis, first, the effect of adsorbent type was studied. The pristine CF, acid-treated CF and CQDs@CF were individually used as adsorbents for BPA preconcentration. Other conditions were as: concentration of BPA 100 ng mL−1, extraction time 30 min, desorption solvent acetonitrile, desorption time 30 min, without pH controlling, and salt addition. According to the results in Fig. 5 A, CQDs@CF showed the highest preconcentration performance compared to the two others. The enhanced adsorption capacity and efficiency of the CQDs@CF sorbent in chromatographic analysis of BPA may have originated from the large surface area and oxygen-containing functional groups created during the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. Thus, the CQDs@CF was selected as an effective sorbent in the optimization of other experimental parameters.

In the next step, the amount of CA used as a CQD precursor was optimized (Fig. 5B). According to the obtained results, 5 mg CA gave the best response, so it was used as the optimal amount in the rest of the stages. Other effective experimental parameters including extraction and desorption parameters, such as extraction time, type of desorption solvent and its volume, desorption time, salt content, and sample solution pH as critical factors were optimized using the one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) method. The effect of extraction time on the extent of BPA loaded on CQDs@CF was investigated in the range of 5 − 40 min, as shown in Fig. 5C. The peak area increases with increasing extraction time and then reaches a relatively steady state. The appearance of the leveling-off point in Fig. 5C depends on the equilibration time of the analyte distribution between the sorbent and the sample solution. According to these results, an appropriate extraction time was set at 20 min.

Mobile phase composition for eluting of BPA in liquid chromatographic (LC) analysis is important and non − polar solvents could be preferred rather than polar solvents. In this study, various solvents, including acetone, methanol, acetonitrile, ethanol, and n-hexane, were examined to optimize the desorption solvent for the target analytes. According to the results (Fig. 5D), the highest signal was obtained in the case of methanol, so it was selected as an optimized desorption solvent. The investigation was conducted on the desorption solvent volume ranging from 0.5 to 2.5 mL to achieve better extraction efficiency. The results in Fig. 5E show that the highest peak areas were obtained using 2 mL of desorption solvent which was found to be the optimal value. In the next experiments, the analyte − loaded modified sorbent was immersed in 2 mL desorption solvent (in a 2 mL vial). Desorption times were also studied with time intervals varying from 5 to 30 min. As shown in Fig. 5F, the highest efficiency was observed for 15 min and remained constant thereafter. According to the obtained results, a desorption time of 15 min was deemed appropriate, and the rest of the experiments continued with this duration. The preconcentration efficiency can be increased or decreased by adding salt to the sample solution, depending on the type of analyte and fiber coating. The partition coefficient of the analyte between the sample solution and the sorbent depends on the ionic strength. On the other hand, any change in ionic strength alters the solution viscosity which affects the diffusion of the analyte in the sample solution. This means that the extraction of the analyte could be remarkably controlled by salt addition from the viewpoint of thermodynamics and kinetics. To examine this parameter, sodium chloride was added to the sample solutions in different concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 % (w/v) to investigate the salting − out effect on extraction efficiency. According to the results, for CQDs@CF (Fig. 5G), the highest extraction efficiency was observed without adding sodium chloride. The addition of NaCl increased the viscosity of the sample, disrupted the interaction between BPA and CQD@CF, and slowed down the extraction process.Additionally, the carbon pores appeared to be occupied by the salt and may have precipitated on its surface coating, which interrupted mass transfer. Thus, the addition of salt was avoided in the next experiments, and this state was considered one of the optimized conditions.

The pH of the solution is another important parameter in sample preparation and preconcentration processes. Based on the chemical structure of the analytes, the pH of the solution can affect the surface structure, surface charge, and chemical stability of the solid phase microextraction adsorbent, as well as the preconcentration of the analytes. The pH of the solution can also change CQDs@CF surface charge altering analyte adsorption yield, which requires to be optimized. So, the extraction efficiency of BPA by CQDs@CF adsorbent was studied over the pH range 3–11 (adjusted by diluted HCl or NaOH). Based on the results (Fig. 5H), the extraction efficiency improved with solution pH in the range 3–6, while, it is depressed at alkaline pH values. These observations could be addressed by resorting to the intermolecular forces occurring at various pHs. It seems that in an acidic solution, the analyte bounds to the adsorbent by only π-π interactions. In contrast, in a neutral solution (pH 6–7), deprotonation of –OH groups the analyte leads to more adsorption by ion–dipole interactions. In strongly alkaline media, BPA exists in a completely deprotonated form which could be repulsed by negatively surface-charged adsorbent, leading to less adsorption. In addition, the CQDs@CF nanocomposite exhibits acceptable stability in neutral media, so pH = 6 was selected as the optimized value.

Applying these optimized parameters improved chromatographic signal intensity. To evaluate the potential of the proposed method in the trace analysis, the quantity of enrichment factor (EF) was calculated. The following equation was used to calculate the analytical figure of merit in terms of EF:
(1)
E F =
C ex
C
o

Here, Cex and Co are the concentrations of the analyte in the extracted phase and the initial aqueous sample. The value of EF was estimated to be 277 (Table 2 ). This satisfactory result could guarantee the method application for the identification of small amounts of the target analyte in real samples. In addition, to evaluate the accuracy of analytical data, the method validation was performed via relative recovery (%RR) tests. The quantity of RR% was obtained using the following equation:
(2)
R R % =
C
found
-
C
real
C
added
× 100

The equation involves three concentrations of the analyte: Creal, Cadded, and Cfound; these represent the original concentration in the real sample, the concentration of the analyte spiked to the real sample, and the analyte concentration in the spiked real sample, respectively. The relative recovery percentages (RR%) lay in the range 93.86 %−100.30 % (Table 3 ). These results confirm the lack of matrix effect on the analytical data indicating the accuracy of the proposed analytical protocol and results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical figures of merit of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Competency analytical figures for the present method under optimal conditions are represented in Table 2. A calibration curve for quantification of the target analyte was constructed by plotting the peak area obtained from the standard solutions versus the corresponding BPA concentration. The proposed SPME method based on a piece of CQDs@CF showed a wide linear response range (LR) over the range 0.05 − 500 ng mL−1 with good linearity (R2 > 0.997), and limit of detection (LOD) equal to 0.01 ng mL−1. The limit of quantification (LOQ) of the method is calculated as 0.04 ng mL−1. In addition, the enrichment factor (EF) (based on the ratio between analyte concentrations in the extracted solution and the initial aqueous sample) was found to be 277. The relative standard deviations (RSD) for a series (n = 5) of newly prepared or fresh CQDs@CF (sorbent to sorbent repeatability) and a certain multi-used (n = 5) CQDs@CF (single sorbent repeatability) evaluated as 4.8 and 7.2 %, respectively. According to the RR value, the responses of the proposed method are not affected by the sample matrix and can be successfully used to measure BPA in synthetic and real samples.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the procedure, real samples were first prepared according to the “preparing real samples” section. These samples included bottled water, packed cup water, packed milk, and thermal paper samples. Next, samples were spiked with BPA at two different levels (10.00 and 50.00 ng mL−1), and the proposed method was used for the measurement of the target analyte. Typical chromatograms obtained for the bottled water real sample before and after spiking 10 ng mL−1 BPA were shown in Fig. 6 (curves I, II), using CQDs@CF sorbent at optimal conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method with others
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs@CF sorbent has several advantages, including simplicity, single − step preparation of coating, no brittleness, long durability without requiring special maintenance conditions, and low cost. The characteristics of the proposed method including the LR, LOD, and RSD of the SPME method based on CQDs@CF sorbent for the determination of BPA were compared (Table 4 ) with other similar methods reported in the literature, [37–42]. The findings of this study indicate that the linear ranges and limit of detection (LOD) obtained are comparable to those achieved by previous methodologies. CQDs@CF serves as a novel sorbent for SPME, which enhances sensitivity and extraction efficiency without incurring additional costs. The approach features a relatively short extraction and analysis time and an acceptable enrichment factor. The favorable analytical outcomes and admitted recoveries demonstrate the effectiveness and suitability of the proposed sorbent for extraction and pre-concentration of BPA. The technique is not influenced by the sample matrix and can be successfully applied to measure BPA in both standard and real samples. Additionally, the CQDs@CF sorbent could be used for the detection of BPA in bottled water, cup water, milk, and thermal paper samples.

In the present study, an innovative and efficient CQDs@CF − based SPME approach was introduced as a novel sorbent offering high surface area, large pore volume, and easy production procedure. The CQDs@CF sorbent with HPLC─UV detection was used to determination of BPA. Some impressive characteristics of the proposed method include wide LR (0.05 − 500 ng mL−1), high EF (277), low LOD (0.01 ng mL−1), and acceptable reproducibility (RSD ≤ 7.2 %) making it a potential technique in real sample analysis. So, the developed method was applied to the determination of trace amounts of BPA (ng mL−1) in bottled water, packaged cup water, packaged milk, and thermal paper samples. The accuracy of analytical results and method validation was ascribed by recovery tests.
Credit author statement

Tahereh Sheikhi (Ph.D. Student): performed the measurements, processed the experimental data, drafted the manuscript and designed the figures, wrote the manuscript, and performed the final revision with input from all authors. Dr. Sirwan Mohammadiazar: contributed to the planning and supervised the work, aided in interpreting the results, and worked on the manuscript. Prof. Habib Razmi: devised the project, the main conceptual ideas, and the proof outline.

Tahereh Sheikhi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Habib Razmi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Sirwan Mohammadiazar: Writing – original draft, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to Azerbaijan Shahid Madani University for financial support of this study.
